questions.explanation: Every MicroProfile Config implementation must support: system properties (priority 400), environment variables (priority 300), and microprofile-config.properties (priority 100).

questions.question: MicroProfile Health's three health check types are:
options: A) Readiness, Liveness, Startup
B) Active, Passive, Scheduled
C) CPU, Memory, Disk
D) HTTP, JMX, Log
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Health defines: @Liveness (is the app alive?), @Readiness (is it ready to serve traffic?), and @Startup (has it finished initialising?).

questions.question: MicroProfile OpenAPI exposes generated documentation at which HTTP endpoint?
options: A) /swagger
B) /openapi
C) /api-docs
D) /wadl
questions.explanation: MicroProfile OpenAPI automatically generates OpenAPI v3 documents and exposes them at the /openapi endpoint.

questions.question: MicroProfile Fault Tolerance provides which resilience annotations?
options: A) @Transactional, @Async, @Schedule
B) @Cached, @Throttled, @RateLimit
C) @Retry, @Fallback, @Timeout, @CircuitBreaker (and others)
D) @Resilient, @Bulkhead, @SafeCall
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Fault Tolerance defines @Retry, @Fallback, @Timeout, @CircuitBreaker, and @Bulkhead for resilient method-level execution.

questions.question: MicroProfile JWT RBAC secures endpoints using:
options: A) Cryptographically signed JWTs carrying user identity and group claims
B) Session cookies and CSRF tokens
C) SOAP WS-Security headers
D) Basic auth with bcrypt-hashed passwords
questions.explanation: MicroProfile JWT RBAC authenticates via a signed JWT containing the user's subject and groups. @RolesAllowed checks those groups for access control.

questions.question: MicroProfile Rest Client allows you to call REST services by:
options: A) Using SOAP WSDL under the hood
B) Writing raw HTTP socket code
C) Defining a JAX-RS-annotated interface — the framework generates the HTTP client implementation
D) Manually constructing JSON payloads with JSON-P
questions.explanation: Annotate an interface with @RegisterRestClient and JAX-RS annotations. MicroProfile generates an HTTP proxy you inject and call like a local bean.

questions.question: MicroProfile OpenTelemetry sends distributed traces to backends such as:
options: A) Grafana and Kibana only
B) Splunk and Datadog exclusively
C) Jaeger, Zipkin, or Instana
D) Prometheus and InfluxDB only
questions.explanation: MicroProfile OpenTelemetry integrates with the CNCF OpenTelemetry project. Traces can be exported to Jaeger, Zipkin, Instana, and other compatible backends.

questions.question: The key distinction between Jakarta EE and MicroProfile application styles is:
options: A) Jakarta EE targets monolithic/web enterprise apps while MicroProfile targets cloud-native microservices
B) Jakarta EE is open source; MicroProfile is commercial
C) Jakarta EE supports only SOAP; MicroProfile supports only REST
D) MicroProfile supersedes all Jakarta EE specifications
questions.explanation: Jakarta EE covers a broad enterprise scope including monolithic .war/.ear deployments, while MicroProfile specifically targets cloud-native microservices. They complement each other.

questions.question: MicroProfile Metrics exposes collected metrics over HTTP in which format(s)?
options: A) JSON or Prometheus format
B) CSV only
C) XML only
D) Binary Protobuf only
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Metrics exposes metrics via REST in either JSON or Prometheus text format, consumable by Prometheus scrapers and tools like Grafana.

questions.question: Jakarta EE and MicroProfile share which values?
options: A) Proprietary licensing and single-vendor governance
B) They share no values — they compete
C) Standardisation, openness, stability, ease of development, and portability
D) Mandatory Docker deployment
questions.explanation: Both Jakarta EE and Eclipse MicroProfile share the values of standardisation, openness, stability, ease of development, and portability.

questions.question: To read a MicroProfile Config value programmatically (not via injection), you use:
options: A) System.getenv('my.property')
B) Config config = ConfigProvider.getConfig(); config.getValue('my.property', String.class)
C) new Properties().load(...).getProperty('my.property')
D) @ConfigProperty(name='my.property') on a field
questions.explanation: ConfigProvider.getConfig() retrieves the runtime Config instance; getValue() fetches a typed value. @ConfigProperty is the declarative injection alternative.

options: A) @RegisterRestClient creates a mock of SwapiService for testing only
B) @RegisterRestClient turns SwapiService into a JMS message producer; @RestClient injects a queue
C) The interface must be a class; interfaces cannot be registered as Rest Clients

D) @RegisterRestClient marks the interface so MicroProfile generates a working HTTP client implementation; @Inject @RestClient injects that generated implementation — calling swapiService.getCharacter('1') makes a real GET HTTP call to the configured URL
questions.explanation: @RegisterRestClient tells MicroProfile Rest Client to generate a proxy implementation for this interface. The base URL is configured in microprofile-config.properties. @Inject @RestClient injects the proxy — method calls on it become real HTTP requests.

options: A) @Retry runs the method in parallel threads; @Timeout stops parallel threads; @Fallback merges results
B) All three annotations do the same thing — they are aliases for each other
C) @Timeout aborts the call if it takes too long; @Retry automatically retries the call on failure; @Fallback calls calculateAmountFallback() if all retries are exhausted or a timeout occurs
D) @Timeout logs timing; @Retry logs retries; @Fallback logs failures
questions.explanation: @Timeout sets a maximum execution time and aborts if exceeded. @Retry retries the method invocation on failure. @Fallback specifies a fallback method (calculateAmountFallback) to call when all retry attempts fail or a non-retryable error occurs — combining three resilience patterns on a single method.

options: A) @Liveness means this check only runs at application startup
B) The /health endpoint returns HTTP 503 with status 'DOWN' and the remaining disk space in MB, because status(false) marks the check as DOWN
C) The method throws an ArithmeticException when freeSpace is 0
D) The /health endpoint returns HTTP 200 with status 'UP' regardless of disk space
questions.explanation: .status(freeSpace > 100) sets status to UP if freeSpace > 100, DOWN otherwise. When DOWN, the /health endpoint returns HTTP 503 Service Unavailable with a JSON body showing status:'DOWN' and the remainingSpace data. @Liveness marks it as a liveness probe (is the app alive?).
